data.title: Principal Financial Group: Share Buybacks Drive Share Value Growth
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

I have found regular success in investing, not in the companies that you see on the shelf at your local grocery store or in the companies that you may know off the tip of your tongue from day-to-day life, but in companies that are in the background. These will be companies that provide services or facilitate other companies' operations without being directly exposed to consumers.

The reason that these companies can often be wonderful investments is that their services are necessary and are not tied to the whims of consumers. This is one reason why I love owning fuel distribution companies, where I can collect revenue from the companies that move fuel from point A to point B without having to be exposed to consumer preference at the convenience store and the top-line risks of the major oil and gas companies.

Today, I want to look at a company that operates in the background. You may never have heard of this company, yet it may directly impact how you interact with the financial and insurance world.

Let's dive in!

Riding The Equity Wave To Bigger Payouts

Principal Financial Group (PFG) is a leading financial services provider. PFG has operated for over 145 years with nearly 20,000 employees in 27 nations. PFG provides 401k and 403b plan oversight and administration for employers, along with annuities and disability insurance plans for individuals. PFG specializes in helping employers and individuals maximize their retirement success via a holistic approach. This has led to strong growth in their assets under management and administration.

As of Q3 2025, PFG had $1.8 trillion of assets under administration and $784 billion in assets under management.

As Kody outlined in a June listicle for Brad Thomas, the plurality of the company's total TTM non-GAAP pre-tax operating earnings came from its Retirement & Income Solutions segment. The remainder was split between the Principal Asset Management segment and the Benefits and Protection segment.

As was the case then, the long-term thesis for PFG looks to still be holding up.

PFG continues to target 9% to 12% annual non-GAAP operating earnings per diluted share growth. For my money, its latest quarterly results demonstrated that this is a realistic growth target. Adjusted EPS surged 13.2% year-over-year to $2.32 in Q3 2025. This was powered by solid results in Specialty Benefits and Retirement & Income Solutions, as well as higher AUM and careful cost management.

Capital allocation to share repurchases, organic capital deployment, strategic acquisitions, and market appreciation remain the growth drivers for PFG. The company's A- S&P credit rating and low payout ratio (more on that in a moment) position it to balance these growth avenues.

That's why I think that the FAST Graphs analyst consensus over the next few years is achievable. In 2025, adjusted EPS is projected to soar by 19.6% to $8.34. For 2026, another 12.2% rise in adjusted EPS to $9.35 is anticipated. In 2027, an additional 10.9% increase in adjusted EPS to $10.37 is predicted.

As shareholders wait for growth to play out, PFG sports a 3.7% dividend yield. The payout ratio should be in the mid-30% to high-30% range for 2025. That should enable PFG to build on its 16-year dividend growth streak.

On the valuation side, PFG looks to be a compelling value right now. At the current $85 price, shares are trading at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 9.3 (13% weight for 2025 and 87% for 2026). This comes in 18% below the 10-year average P/E ratio of 11.3 and my fair value estimate of $104 per share.

If PFG reverts to fair value and lives up to growth forecasts, it could generate a 28% total return by the end of 2026. Through 2030, PFG could post 15% annual total returns.

PFG's valuation arguably prices in its risks. Still, it's worth highlighting a few noteworthy risks facing the company. These include the potential for equity/bond market downturns and incidents that carry reputational risk to the firm.

Equity markets have been on a tear now for years. Of course, they don't go up in a straight line. Along the way, there will be volatility that could temporarily reduce PFG's AUM and fee income.

The other risk to the company is that it must maintain a positive brand reputation. If its funds can't outperform their benchmarks or an event happens to diminish client trust (e.g., a significant cyber breach), this could result in a mass client exodus. That would also make it more difficult for PFG to attract new clients.

Conclusion

PFG is an attractive behind-the-scenes opportunity to expand your dividend growth portfolio. Having a yield that's well above the market index and a stellar track record of growth allows PFG to be able to find a home in a portfolio looking for strong capital appreciation and dividend growth together.

These are the kinds of investments that we look for within Dividend Kings. Enjoying both dividend growth and capital appreciation on one side, while also having the other side of our portfolio focused on more immediate income-style opportunities that will provide you with greater levels of yield today, while the other half of our portfolio provides you with greater yields tomorrow.

When a farmer plants a field, they rarely do so with the plan for that field to be all the produce they will ever collect. They plant a field so that next year they can plant it again and enjoy an even more bountiful crop. As a dividend investor, I'm not interested in buying investments that are going to diminish in value or provide diminishing income because they pour it all out instead of having a plan to grow it. My entire portfolio and my individual investments are aligned with my goal of collecting a bountiful harvest today and an even larger harvest tomorrow.

Plant your seeds. Watch them grow. Enjoy a bountiful harvest.

data.title: Dropbox: A Mispriced Cash Machine With Hidden Upside
data.description:
Introduction

At a time where tech investors chase explosive AI-driven growth stories at nosebleed multiples, Dropbox (DBX) has quietly transformed itself into something far rarer: a mature, highly profitable cash compounder trading at an attractive valuation. While revenues are essentially flat, the real story underneath is one of expanding margins and aggressive share repurchases that the market is currently assigning almost zero value to. At just 10.0x earnings and 7.9x EV/EBITDA, roughly half the multiple of its peers, I think Dropbox is one of the few places in software today where you can wait for either a continuation of its “steady-eddie” cash generation or the pleasant surprise of a return to growth.

Recent Results

Dropbox’s third quarter came above expectations with a beat on both the top and bottom line. During the quarter, the company’s revenue fell 0.7% to $634.4 million surpassing estimates by $10.8 million but when excluding the dragging impact from FormSwift, the company’s revenue actually would have been higher year over year (150bps impact).

On the revenue side, the company’s annual recurring revenue, or ARR, stood at $2.54 billion, reflecting a 1.7% drop compared to last year with FormSwift contributing to the bulk of that headwind. The company ended the quarter with 18.07 million paying users, down about 64k sequentially, mainly due to downsells in managed accounts and reduced investment in FormSwift. That said, this was offset by gains from the lower-priced Simple plan aimed at mobile users.

Despite the revenue declines, the company’s retention across individual and self-serve Teams plans has been steady and the real kicker has come from margin upside. For example, one of the things that stood out to me was that even though gross margin compressed to 81.4% (down 270bps year over year) due to higher depreciation from the data center refresh cycle and infrastructure investment for Dash, the company’s operating margins actually expanded. At quarter end, operating margins clocked in at 41.1%, up 490bps compared to last year thanks to lower headcount and marketing spend, while free cash flow came in at $293.7 million for a 46.3% margin). Overall, even through we probably have a steady, no growth business here on the top line, I’d say that management is executing well given their tight cost control ahead of Q4 Dash marketing investments.

As a result of the improved profitability, EPS of $0.74 surpassed Wall Street estimates by 9 cents and the company’ s leverage metrics have improved. On the balance sheet, Dropbox closed the quarter with $925 million in cash that provides ample liquidity. In addition to that, the company has drawn $1.15 billion under its term loan facility with an additional $1.55 billion available including a recent $700 million delayed draw amendment intended to retire upcoming convertible notes without incremental interest expense this year. Given the ‘steady eddie’ nature of the underlying business, net leverage of 1.7x (or 2.5x on a gross basis) looks manageable for Dropbox.

Finally, with a business that’s spitting off cash, Dropbox has turned to share repurchases as a preferred method of allocating capital. During the quarter, the company repurchased about 14 million shares for $390 million with another $1.58 billion remaining under its authorization after a new $1.5 billion program was approved. This is a strong confidence indicator in my book that suggests that management views their shares as undervalued. It also means that with a flat growing business that repurchases its shares at a low valuation, EPS can actually grow as the share count (the denominator in EPS) reduces over time.

Dropbox is making the right moves scaling Dash and strengthening Self-Serve

Q3 marks the first quarter of the self-serve Dash launch, now available via Dropbox.com at $19 per seat per month with a 50% first-year discount for existing customers. On the conference call post-quarter, management noted that engagement is encouraging, as 60% of managed Dash users use the product at least two days per week, indicating it is becoming embedded in weekly workflows. The company continues to prioritize adoption over near-term monetization, with the integration of Dash into Dropbox Business now underway. Dropbox frames Dash as a multi-year expansion of Dropbox’s TAM rather than an immediate revenue driver, with traction expected to build through 2026.

Another point that was hammered home was the fact that the company’s core FFS (it’s self-serve File, Sync and Share) is showing signs of stabilization. While I’d note that the total ARR of $2.54 billion I mentioned earlier is lower compared to last year, this quarter did mark the smallest sequential drop this year. Ex-FormSwift, ARR growth is slightly positive compared to last year and so the core business isn’t seeing as much decline as the headline numbers suggest. While paying users are flat, ARPU is slightly up sequentially at $139.07 mix shift to higher-priced plans. Given the ongoing benefits from simplified cancellation flows and localized trial messaging, that’s helping improve retention and conversion trends across both individual and team cohorts.

For Q4, Dropbox is guiding for revenue to $627.5 million at the midpoint (down 2.5% year over year) and operating margin to 37%, consistent with planned Dash investments and post-refresh depreciation effects. For FY’25, guidance was raised to $2.52 billion at the midpoint (down 1.4% year over year), with operating margin now expected at 40%, 100 bps higher compared to the previous guide. On free cash flow, they expect they should see unlevered FCF “at or above $1B”, as indicated in the table below. To me, this upward revision on margins in particular signals confidence in stabilizing the core business through better retention and self-serve traction, but it’s still likely that FormSwift will remain a drag. While management hasn’t commented on it publicly, I do think that at some point it will make sense to sell this piece of the business, raise cash, and reallocate to more growthier initiatives.

With more than three quarters of the year out of the way, Dropbox expects FY’26 will be a year of margin expansion as they lap their Oct-24 RIF and continue to invest in Dash and strengthening self-serve teams business. As we look ahead to next year, I don’t think it’s out of question for the company to see a return to revenue growth.

One quote from the Q&A that stands out is CEO Andrew Houston's analogy to Netflix's (NFLX) shift from DVD mailing to streaming where he said "we see parallels between our File Sync business and organizing all your cloud content and connecting AI to your work context we see both a natural evolution of the value we're already providing to our core users and then we're able to unlock new generations of Dropbox users who for one reason or another aren't using files in Dropbox today". That to me hints that there is long-term expansion through Dash. Even it’s only successful on the margin, I think the good news for investors is that the market isn’t assigning any premium to Dropbox’s future growth initiatives; it’s being valued as a business with minimal future prospects.

Valuation assumes no growth

When we look at what the sellside thinks of Dropbox, it’s clear that there is a significant bearish bias. Of the 11 analysts, there is only one buy rating coming out of RBC Capital markets with a $38 target price. However, with 6 holds and 3 sell ratings, the average price target of $29 implies that shares are likely overvalued with return potential of -2.9% over the next year (source: Bloomberg).

I’m taking the other side of that. While so many are focused on a business that’s dying here with nothing to show for, I think it’s helpful to zoom out. From the pre-pandemic period, Dropbox went from a money losing company burning hundreds of millions of dollars to a business that’s now putting up a billion dollars of unlevered free cash flow. While it’s indeed possible that Dropbox’s revenues stay flat forever, there’s a real business here and one with a management team that’s proven to investors that they’re serious about improving margins.

The Dropbox of today boasts enormous scale. With over 700 million registered users, it has over a trillion pieces of content. Having penetrated Fortune 500 customers with over 18 million paying users through a self-service model, the market is only focused on cash flows of yesterday and not of tomorrow. There’s real value in the IP and intangibles on this business (albeit hard to assess) but certainly more than $9 billion when you’ve got a business running more than a billion dollars in high FCF conversion EBITDA.

Compared to peers, Dropbox isn’t expensive. At 10.0x P/E and 7.9x EV/EBITDA, it trades at roughly half the balance sheet of peers in the space (source: Bloomberg). Some will say that that’s warranted but shareholders will earn a ~10% shareholder yield in 2026 as the company steps up its share repurchase plan. As well, Dropbox has rarely been cheaper, trading at a substantial discount to its historical average multiples.

So where are the risks?

It’s worth thinking about how an investment like this could go wrong. Firstly, it’s certainly not lost on me that competition from deep pocketed mega-vendors and a long tail of other competitors may inhibit growth. Dropbox has established itself as a category leader over the past decade but persistent competitive pressures from a long tail of competitors could inhibit the company from maintaining top-line growth, expanding margins, and retaining customers. Additionally, Dropbox may find difficulty competing with mega-vendors such as Microsoft (MSFT) and Google (GOOG), which can afford to heavily discount or give away file sync and share functionality as an add-on to a broader productivity suite.

There’s also the risk of commoditization of the underlying functionality. While Dropbox is a multi layered platform, its underlying storage functionality faces commoditization and therefore higher degrees of pricing power and profitability may be difficult to achieve. Ultimately, I’d like to see this evolve and adapt to focus on SMBs and roll out functional offerings through bundled products and services. But as the business stands today, it could be susceptible to commoditization which would put pressure on the margin profile that’s key to the bullish thesis.

Conclusion

Despite some of the risks, I think most of these are already priced into the stock and Dropbox offers good value in what I see is a pretty overvalued market in the tech space with respect to AI. With stable core retention, expanding margins, disciplined capital allocation, and the early potential of Dash, I think management at Dropbox is executing far better than its “no-growth” label suggests. At today’s valuation, investors are paying very little for a business generating substantial free cash flow and almost nothing for future upside. As such, I think it’s an undervalued and unloved stock so I’m a buyer of shares today.

data.title: EOG Resources: Attractive Entry Point For A Low-Cost, High-Quality Energy Leader
data.description:
Introduction

Last time I covered EOG Resources (EOG), I highlighted their strong margins, robust cash flows, and very solid financials, with a multi-basin US strategy with an international expansion with natural gas on top.

The stock fell a bit since the initial analysis was published, while the company recorded an even better than expected quarter, now seeing higher cash flows for the entire year than I initially assumed, while the macro environment advanced in a potentially favorable direction for oil and gas.

Internal Developments

EGO reported a solid Q3, with $1.4 billion in FCF during the quarter and $3.7 billion over the first 9 months of 2025. They also increased their FCF guidance to $4.5 billion ($65 WTI and $3.5 HH) thanks to the successful integration of Encino and reductions in costs, including a target of low single-digit percentage drop in well costs this year, while also continuing to expand internationally (UAE and Bahrain recently). This would place them at a P/FCF ratio of ~12.7, which is not bad, but keep in mind that oil prices can continue to fall and drag them down as well.

Based on their latest report, we see that EOG remained in an excellent financial position, with current assets well above the current liabilities and still a ton of cash available now that the ~$5.6 billion Encino deal ($3.5 billion of debt and $2.1 billion of cash on hand) was closed. This is ultimately a big advantage, as it’s not very common to see an oil and gas producer with a net cash position in this environment, with oil prices down and also undergoing an expansion.

EOG continued to spend almost everything on shareholder returns, with 89% of the $4.5 billion FCF estimated for 2025 going on dividends and buybacks so far, with an 8% increase in regular dividend in 2025 and $1.8 billion spent on buybacks as of Q3.

Again, I’m not really a fan of dividends or buybacks no matter what, even though I see some good value in buying the stock itself, especially since there are a ton of good buying opportunities in the oil and gas market right now. I’d much rather see them continue to expand than do this, although they replied to a question during the Q3 Earnings Release as follows:

(...) The reason we came out with that 70% commitment of free cash flow return to shareholders that it's durable throughout the cycle. But as you know, you've seen we've basically exceeded that in the last few years, been closer to about 90%, I think low -- maybe 92% of free cash flow return to shareholders. Going forward, we love where our balance sheet is right now. Our total debt is right at our target of total debt versus EBITDA at bottom cycle prices at about 1x. And I think we're in a great spot with our cash position (...) with $5.5 billion of liquidity, it gives us a lot of opportunities to continue to invest throughout the cycle or look for small bolt-ons or other opportunities to build value for the shareholders. I wouldn't say that it's a priority to continue to build that cash balance at all. (...) right now, we actually see continued return of cash to shareholders through stock buybacks as being a pretty opportunistic avenue that we have in front of us, not only for EOG, but really for the entire sector right now.

Macro Developments

I’ll begin once again with one of the most important near-term catalysts that advanced in a positive direction, with a rate cut seen in late October and expectations for a third one in December, although that hangs on a lot of uncertainty now. Again, similar to their peers, EOG should benefit from these cuts since they’d make debt cheaper - especially newer one, which can be used for future developments.

The company’s total debt increased following the closing of the recent deal, while these cuts can also benefit the entire industry and economy as a whole by boosting economic activity, leading to more demand for oil and gas, as their partners and final consumers would enjoy lower costs of debt as well and see increased discretionary budgets, boosting spending and investing. Again, this is ultimately a key part of why the central banks use this tool, with hikes being meant to slow down the economy to fight inflation and cuts doing the opposite (with their timing and pace being very important).

Of course, this won’t happen overnight, while the near-term future of these cuts remains uncertain as the US Government just went through its longest shutdown in history, causing the BLS to delay its reporting significantly, with recent reports relatively mixed but positive expectations from Fed officials regarding a December cut. These helped the market recover a bit, but the fear remained especially due to Big Tech’s AI spending and profitability worries, although this could also hint at a repositioning in the market into other assets - like gold/oil/etc. stocks.

One key thing to pay attention to will be how the US-Venezuela situation plays out. For context, the US placed an aircraft carrier in the Caribbean and struck dozens of ships there as of recently, with the US planning to designate a Venezuelan drug cartel it alleges is led by Venezuela’s president as a foreign terrorist organization and international airlines canceling Venezuela flights. Trump even hinted that this move could allow the US to target certain assets and infrastructure in the country, despite also saying that he’s not planning an attack but also saying that Maduro’s days are numbered, with fear and uncertainty remaining very high.

As of the moment of writing this, the most recent development is Colombia’s president pointing out what I believe to be the obvious fact that this pressure is about oil, not drugs, and Ukraine claiming that Russia sent a top general in charge of Russia's “Equator Task Force” to train the country’s army. This comes as Russia had previously said that it’s “ready to help” Venezuela’s military - which has been rallying troops recently - while Maduro urged Trump to avoid a “forever war” in the region, similar to the one in Afghanistan.

As I said, I believe this has the potential to degenerate into something that can lead to even unprecedented disruptions in the oil market, even though Venezuela is far from the biggest producer. This is more about what this company means for oil and OPEC than what it produces, as Venezuela is not just a random target. Venezuela, one of the founding members of OPEC, has the most proven oil reserves on the planet, with roughly 4 times what the US or Russia have - or nearly twice the two combined, so it’s not hard to guess why anyone would want control over it (directly or through a “friendly” government).

There’s also been some news regarding the war in Ukraine, with a new draft proposed by the US, with Ukraine agreeing to most of it with some “minor” details to settle. It’s not the end - and we’ve seen a big attack coming from Russia the day after this news came - but it’s a step in that direction.

However, keep in mind that this war (and expectations of it) was after all a huge factor in oil’s jump from ~$65 in December 2021 to nearly $120 in March 2022, so a reversal over time can add further pressure on oil prices. Although I don’t expect Russia to be back at the same levels as before in the global trade overnight, we have to think about two things: first, an end to this war would allow them to focus their efforts (and money) back on commodity producing, which can disrupt global markets; and second, the market’s expectations would evolve based on the news, which is ultimately a key factor behind the price of oil and especially gas and a reason why it’s so volatile.

Also, something that can put a lot of pressure on Russia to turn to the negotiation table were Trump’s recent sanctions on Russian oil, which immediately made Chinese state oil buyers stop buying and pushed the country’s other buyers into reducing it. Almost immediately, we’ve seen this basically forcing Lukoil - one of Russia’s largest oil companies - into selling their international assets, while some countries even seized their local assets, with the administration saying that the sanctions hit as intended, cutting a significant source of revenue for Russia’s war - and it’s honestly surprising it took nearly 4 years since the war began to see this.

Around the same time, we’ve seen OPEC and its allies agreeing to boost their oil production in December, but decided to halt its increases in Q1 2026 at least, which can be a very significant boost to oil prices going forward, as OPEC’s production increases combined with tariff uncertainty and other macro pressure were key in oil prices falling this year.

At the same time, recent data about US inventories and OPEC shifting its forecast caused oil prices to fall recently, which comes as a new report showed rising crude inventories in the US. OPEC said that “certain international media outlets distorted the data presented in OPEC’s Monthly Oil Market Report,” but this continued to fuel the market’s fear that the global supply is more than enough to cover the current demand (which is not great for the price), putting some pressure on the price as a result, and we’ve seen JP Morgan even saying a few days ago that oil could even fall to $30 in 2027. I don’t see these forecasts as guaranteed things (the same JP Morgan said oil could go to $100 in 2024), but they give some food for thought and offer good insights.

At the end of the day, oil remains a very volatile commodity, and it will continue to depend on OPEC, the global economy, market’s opinions and expectations, which is why I believe EOG’s exposure to natural gas can be a great advantage, offering a lot of potential in the future and even a safety net in today’s environment, as natural gas prices still stood strong while oil fell. This domestic and international diversification definitely adds long-term potential for hedging, which is something they also mentioned during the Q3 report:

We are especially excited about the potential for international unconventional development through our entry into the UAE and Bahrain. Our differentiated exposure to both North American liquids and natural gas as well as international unconventionals positions EOG to benefit from medium- and long-term growth in all 3 areas, creating multiple avenues for future value creation.

Overall, although I remain bullish on EOG, I believe we should remain aware of the potential near-term pressure especially on the stock price, as the market’s opinion around oil stocks can easily deteriorate even more, while delays in rate cuts or various macro factors can keep hurting oil prices, potentially affecting the company’s cash flows directly.

Updated Valuation

Starting with the updated FCF of $4.5 billion in 2025 (assuming $65 WTI and $3.5 HH), I’ll assume a 5% CAGR over the first 5 years thanks to further cost savings, synergies and rate cuts, with a 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2% terminal growth rate. I’ll go once again with a WACC of 8.5% (above the current assumptions of 7.52%; assumptions: ~8% cost of equity, ~4.15% cost of debt), with $4.16 billion in net debt now that the deal was closed and 544 million shares outstanding. This gets us to an enterprise value of $81.91 billion, an equity value of $77.75 billion and an intrinsic value of $142.92 per share.

Conclusions

I reiterate EOG’s Buy rating, with a solid financial position, a diversified portfolio with solid assets and an attractive valuation, with potential coming from their international expansion and cost savings. The company should be resilient even in a lower oil environment, but risks and volatility remain since they’re always going to depend on the commodity, of course.

data.title: How I'm Investing Ahead Of America's Next Manufacturing Supercycle
data.description:
Introduction

I love the quote below:

"Those who have knowledge, don't predict. Those who predict don't have knowledge." - Lao Tzu

And this one as well:

"It's tough to make predictions, especially about the future." - Yogi Berra

The funny thing about these quotes is that they are right, as much as it pains me to admit that. Predicting the future is impossible, as not a single human being on earth has the ability to look into the future. There are simply too many variables involved. Interestingly enough, as I wrote in May, not even hedge funds, which are supposed to be the smart guys on the market, are able to beat the market.

In my article, I highlighted a $1 million bet from Warren Buffett. In 2008, he made the case that hedge funds would fail to beat the market over a multi-year period. In 2016, hedge funds had returned 22.0%. The S&P 500 had returned roughly 85%.

A 2020 study revealed the same, as it showed that close to 90% of finance professionals failed to beat the market on a 15-year and 10-year period. I highlighted the numbers in the table below, which were also part of my May 1 article.

When it comes to making predictions, I try to play it safe. By that, I don’t mean that my investments come without risk, but that I do not turn my portfolio into an event-driven trading vehicle. That’s not what I’m about. Nor do I ever plan on changing that.

As I wrote on November 9 in an article titled "The Most Important Investment Lessons I’ve Ever Shared," I only invest in high-quality companies and improve my odds by looking for opportunities very far out the Equity Yield Curve, which is basically a concept based on market inefficiency caused by the rather short-term mindset of most traders/investors.

That’s what nearly all of my Big Picture ideas are based on, including the bullish energy thesis, which looks beyond current oversupply, my bullish cyclical value thesis, my entire AI infrastructure thesis, and so many more.

Generally speaking, I try to go beyond building a thesis for a company that’s simply based on XYZ having a good dividend growth track record or terrific business model. While that is extremely important, I want to create a long-term edge that can help me/us generate alpha.

In this article, I’ll elaborate on a thesis I have never spent more than a few lines on. That thesis connects the AI revolution to economic re-shoring and cyclical growth. Heck, to make it more complicated, politics are also a part of it.

It’s a long-term thesis that I expect to take shape over the next 5-10 years and have a major influence on a wide number of industries, including manufacturing.

So, let’s get right to it!

Manufacturing Has Suffered

America’s economy hasn’t done poorly in recent decades.

On the contrary.

As we can see below, the gross domestic product has grown from $20 trillion during the pandemic to $30.4 trillion within the span of just five years. During the Great Financial Crisis, the GDP was roughly half of what it is now. By any measure, that’s a great performance.

Unfortunately, manufacturing wasn’t a big part of it. The chart below can be perceived as very depressing for many industrial areas, as a long-term surge in manufacturing output ended during the Great Financial Crisis. It never recovered. Even worse, it has been trending lower since 2019.

To add a third chart to this series, total manufacturing accounts for less than 10.0% of total GDP. Prior to the Great Financial Crisis, that number was 13.0%. To add some numbers, a quick back-of-the-napkin calculation shows that if manufacturing still accounted for 13.0% of GDP, it would be contributing $4.0 trillion to the economy. However, the real number is just $2.9 trillion, which is a difference of $1.1 trillion.

So, where did that capital go?

For starters, output didn’t necessarily vanish. While places like the Rust Belt (here’s a great paper from 2015) have seen a substantial decline in output, total production remained flat, which means producers at home became more efficient and excess demand mostly went overseas. That’s where the growth happened, as nations like China were (and still are) simply much cheaper.

That’s also one of the reasons why President Trump has been talking about tariffs for many years, even before he had any plans of running for president in 2016. Luckily for the market, tariff fears are now largely gone, as the U.S. and China have reached an agreement at 10%, which is a mile below the triple-digit rates that were temporarily applied this year.

However, that’s just the start.

America is eager to bring back manufacturing, yet China is also eager to maintain its leadership. They are so eager that they have fully focused on technology to increase their resilience against tariffs.

China Is Going Into Overdrive To Keep Its Manufacturing Crown

Tariffs are nothing but an additional tax on a good and/or service.

This means that one way to lower tariff risks is to simply produce more efficiently. For example, if tariffs are 10% but I can produce 10% more efficiently, the risk has been mitigated. I’m painting with a broad brush, but I think you get the idea.

That’s what China has been working on for a long time, as The Wall Street Journal wrote in an in-depth article titled "Robots and AI Are Already Remaking the Chinese Economy."

A few years ago, I thought Robots were an overhyped topic. I didn’t pay attention, as I simply didn’t care. This year, I changed that, as I believe the combination of AI innovation and robots turns this into a tangible technology, so to speak.

As it turns out, China has been a leader for quite some time in this field, as the WSJ noted that the country has moved way beyond basic automation to the implementation of full-blown "factory brains," which are systems that manage entire production lines and logistics. That’s China’s way of remaining the world’s factor despite rising costs.

To give you some numbers, the country installed close to 300,000 robots last year. That’s roughly nine times as many as the U.S. and the rest of the world combined. Last year, it had more than 2 million operational robots. That’s roughly half of the population of Los Angeles.

Now, it’s home to 45 of the most advanced factories on the planet (out of 131).

The interesting thing is that China isn’t worried about replacing human labor. That’s because of its horrible demographics. As some of you may know, its one-child policy ruined its demographics. The chart below perfectly displays the expected population decline.

One could even say China is forced to automate if it’s indeed about to lose hundreds of millions of potential workers in the decades ahead.

That’s where "dark factories" come in, which are exactly what you think they are.

Dark factories are facilities that are entirely automated. They can basically run without lights or a break room for employees, so to speak. TexSpaceToday focused on this a bit more, as it noted that machines take on every task, which includes assembling stuff, inspecting it, and managing the internal logistics. It’s essentially a big black box where economic activity occurs without humans. According to this source, it’s a plan that started a decade ago:

This transformation is driven by the “Made in China 2025” initiative, launched in 2015 to make China a high-tech manufacturing powerhouse. The strategy emphasizes robotics, AI, and smart factories, with the government investing heavily in subsidies and infrastructure. - TexSpaceToday

Going back to the WSJ article, it noted a few case studies where this has turned into a big success:

  • The appliance maker Miidea has increased revenue per employee by 40% between 2015 and 2024, as it reduced some processes from 15 minutes to 30 seconds.
  • The apparel producer Bosideng reduced the time it takes to produce a sample from 100 days to just 27 days. Development costs fell by 60%.
  • Huawei and Conch Group, which produces cement, created systems to assess clinker strength with much higher accuracy and reduce energy consumption.

Huawei also launched something called "PortGPT." As you may have guessed, it’s a model to automate ports. In places like Tianjin, the port now requires 60% fewer workers. Close to 90% of all large container equipment is automated. Think about that for a second.

Based on this context, I think it’s a very impressive endeavour, as China is achieving at least 2 goals here. While I’m not sure what goal enjoys the highest priority, one of them is that it addresses its horrible demographics. Automation will do wonders for long-term efficiency and prevent potential labor shortfalls in the future.

By doing so, it can achieve its second goal, which is to remain competitive. While I am painting with a super broad brush here, the further it cuts operating costs, the more protected it is against low labor costs in other nations (think of Vietnam) and U.S. tariffs.

Moreover, I would even make the case that China has done a lot of de-risking, as it controls a lot of commodities (including rare earths) and has automated processes.

So far, so good. What’s more important is what this means for us as investors.

Now What? (The Bigger Picture)

I thought for a few days about just how big a topic this is for the market.

For me, as someone with a background in supply chain studies, it shows just how important this is in the context of geopolitics. It’s also critical for Big Tech. Huawei, which I just mentioned, has basically become an operating system for industry companies. This makes tech companies critical to achieve efficiency gains and as geopolitical players.

In the U.S., we are now seeing a closer collaboration with Big Tech and the government, as CNN reported a few days ago:

Under the new Genesis Mission, created by executive order, the Department of Energy will develop a new AI platform that uses federal scientific data to train AI models and agents made for scientific research. It underscores President Donald Trump’s focus on AI in his second term, coming after he’s introduced a sweeping package of initiatives and policy recommendations in July called the AI Action Plan.

The Genesis Mission aims to take the tech and business industries’ progress in AI and apply it to scientific research in health, energy, manufacturing and other fields, Department of Energy Secretary Chris Wright said on a call with reporters on Monday. He also said the program would lower energy prices for consumers, a key challenge as investment in AI have increased this year. - CNN

This collaboration is important, as America does not have the flexibility that China has. They cannot simply automate ports and similar facilities. Earlier this year, when President Biden was still in office, he had to deal with a dockworker strike, as you may remember. They asked for major pay boosts and more job security. They got it, supported by strong unions.

My point isn’t that they do not deserve this (good for them and their families), but that this structure makes it hard to automate. Again, I support this, as I don’t want to see people lose their ability to feed their families and build their version of the American dream just so we can save a few bucks in the supply chain.

No, my point is that other measures need to be taken in order to compete with China.

This brings me back to Big Tech, as America has the giants that compete with Huawei. Tesla (TSLA), for example, is the biggest player in humanoid robots in the U.S. Here’s what it said during its latest earnings call:

I think it's important to emphasize that Tesla really is the leader in real-world AI. No one can do what we can do with real-world AI. [...] We're also on the cusp of something really tremendous with Optimus, which I think is likely to be or has potential to be the biggest product of all time. [...] I'm not aware of any robot program by Ford or GM or any U.S. sort of car companies. [...] we do have the ingredients of real-world AI and exceptional electrical, mechanical engineering capabilities and the ability to scale production, which I don't think anyone else has all of those ingredients. - TSLA 3Q25 Earnings Call

I like the reference to the "infinite money glitch," which basically describes the foundation of dark factories:

Optimus at scale is the infinite money glitch. It's like this is -- it's difficult to express the magnitude of -- like if you've got something like that -- like if Optimus, I think, probably achieve 5x the productivity of a person per year because it can operate 24/7, it doesn't even need to charge. It can operate at tethered. [...] That's why I call the infinite money glitch. - TSLA 3Q25 Earnings Call

Needless to say, this is still a process in its early stages. As Tesla made clear, there’s a supply chain for cars, a supply chain for computers, but no supply chain for humanoids. While 2026 will see the first mass production (although on a smaller scale), TSLA aims for an output of at least 1 million units in the years ahead.

And then there’s NVIDIA (NVDA), which is becoming the industrial operating system of the West.

Physical AI is already a multibillion-dollar business addressing a multitrillion dollar opportunity on the next leg of growth for NVIDIA. Leading U.S. manufacturers and robotics innovators are leveraging NVIDIA's 3 computer architecture to train on NVIDIA, test on Omniverse's computer and deploy real-world AI and just in robotic computers. - NVDA 3Q26 Earnings Call

It’s all about physical AI:

The transition to agentic and physical AI will be revolutionary, giving rise to new applications, companies, products and services. [...] As you consider infrastructure investments, consider these 3 fundamental dynamics [...] The transition to accelerated computing [...] The transition to generative AI [...] And the transition to agentic and physical AI. - NVDA 3Q26 Earnings Call

They even have something called a Digital Twin, which is a system where a company’s entire operation (i.e., a factory or a cargo ship) is modeled. The model is then used to simulate scenarios, including design streamlining, better processes, cost savings, and other related things.

As you can imagine, the addition of AI and what feels like unlimited computing power can turn this into overdrive.

Another good example is Rockwell Automation (ROK), which is seeing strong demand from e-commerce and warehousing optimization, economic re-shoring, and a much bigger focus on general efficiency gains, including digital twin systems (see below).

Generally speaking, these are the companies that will likely win in America, as they have competitive moats. However, they are not alone, as they need better infrastructure, like grid modernization. That’s where Eaton Corp (ETN) and Quanta Services (PWR) come in. While they aren’t cheap, I believe they will be the biggest winners of the industrial buildout.

And, as most readers will know, they have a ton of overlap with the AI infrastructure thesis, as it’s all connected. AI, re-shoring, the geopolitical arms race with China, it’s all coming together.

Essentially, without the equipment and services (ETN and PWR), the buildout isn’t possible. Then there’s Jacobs Solutions (J), which is a company employing a lot of engineers to work directly with customers and improve manufacturing.

And, personally, some of my favorites are transportation companies.

If I am right on this thesis, we could see a manufacturing renaissance in the U.S. It could lead to output growth for the first time since the Great Financial Recession and potentially add trillions to manufacturing output.

This requires transportation. It’s one of the reasons why I have spent the past three years (during super weak cyclical growth) buying Canadian Pacific Kansas City (CP), Union Pacific (CP), and Old Dominion Freight Line (ODFL).

  • CP is the first railroad connecting all three North American nations. It benefits from economic re-shoring, China’s move into Mexico to mitigate tariff risks, and its improved connections between supply and demand hubs all over North America.

  • UNP dominates the Western two-thirds of the U.S. with Berkshire-owned BNSF. It is now in the process of buying Norfolk Southern (NSC), which will likely create the first transcontinental Class I railroad of the United States.

  • Old Dominion Freight Line (ODFL) and its peers like XPO (XPO) and SAIA (SAIA), which I like as well, are less-than-truckload companies. They basically connect various clients in the same trucks. This gives them an edge, as it’s very complicated to run an efficient LTL network, due to being reliant on advanced warehouses and demand prediction. In an environment where factories become automated, I expect a lot more demand for transportation and more flexibility. After all, these advanced factories will have minimal inventories (inventories are dead capital) and rely on frequent supplies. That’s perfect for LTL companies that can service a wide range of clients on a very frequent basis.

Needless to say, there are many more ideas. However, as I’m breaking the 3,000-word count again, I’m going to focus on more actionable ideas in the weeks ahead. For now, what matters is that I believe we are dealing with a massive long-term opportunity here.

Takeaway

I love discussing Big Picture ideas. In this case, we got to discuss a theme that combines geopolitics, macroeconomics, and secular growth from AI. In this case, China is leading the way by going all-in on automation to deal with poor demographics and to make its manufacturing base bulletproof.

The U.S. will have to follow. However, it’s hard, as there’s a lot of regulation and employee protection (which is a good thing for many). This means technology will have to play a bigger role with a focus on labor productivity. When combining tariffs, America’s large resource base, and its technology leaders, I am extremely bullish on the future of manufacturing.

I even expect a manufacturing renaissance over the next 5-10 years.

I’m so convinced that I’m willing to stake my reputation and portfolio on it, given substantial investments in transportation and other cyclical companies.

While short-term benefits will be hardly visible, I believe that if we pick the right stocks, we could be part of what I expect to become an American manufacturing renaissance.

That’s also why I will provide a lot more food for thought, including higher-yielding ideas for the income-focused investors among us.

Exciting times are ahead(!).

data.title: W.P. Carey: The Quiet REIT Comeback? Strong Q3 And Momentum Could Set The Stage For 2026
data.description:
Introduction

I last covered W.P. Carey (WPC) roughly 6 months ago after they reported their Q1 earnings. Although they missed on their top & bottom lines, AFFO and revenue actually grew year-over-year.

In their latest quarter, the REIT actually beat on both their top & bottom lines and again saw higher growth from the previous year. And with tailwinds from lower base rates likely going into 2026, WPC looks poised to deliver solid results, should we manage to dodge a recession.

Although I remain cautious due to economic uncertainty and would like to see how most of 2026 plays out before considering them a buy, WPC's below peer-average valuation and well-covered dividend yield over 5% appear attractive.

And while I think any significant upside will likely be limited near-term, WPC could see solid price appreciation over the next 24 to 36 months.

In this article, I discuss WPC's latest earnings, fundamentals, and why the stock remains attractive despite lingering risks.

Previous Hold Thesis

I last covered W.P. Carey this past June in an article titled: Dividend Raise Gives Me Confidence, But Headwinds Keep Me Cautious.

The REIT successfully transitioned from office properties in 2024 and managed to see solid AFFO and revenue growth. Despite their improving fundamentals, their ongoing tenant headwinds and macro uncertainty raised some concerns, increasing the likelihood for underperformance.

Since, the stock has seen some solid price appreciation, up roughly 7%. They've trailed the S&P (SP500) by roughly half, who's up close to 14% over the same period.

Solid Q3 & Momentum

W.P. Carey reported their Q3 earnings and delivered solid results. Despite AFFO declining from the prior quarter, it managed to beat analysts' estimates by $0.04. AFFO amounted to $1.25, down from $1.28 in Q2. But year-over-year this rose a solid 5.9%.

Revenue amounted to $431.3 million, beating by $6.51 million and rising from $430.8 million the previous year's quarter. The drop in AFFO from Q2 could be attributed to a temporary decline in occupancy as a result of known move-outs.

Occupancy stood at 97%. Management stated the decline was temporary and that re-leasing efforts are already underway. Revenue increased due to an $8 million increase in rental revenue, thanks to the company's embedded rent escalators.

As a result, same-store rent growth was solid at 2.4% for the quarter with 2.5% expected for the remainder of the year. Going into 2026, management stated they expected to see around 2%.

Investment Activity & Guidance

As far as investment activity, WPC saw solid activity with $656.4 million in Q3. Year-to-date these stood at $1.65 billion at an average cap rate of 7.6%. These were mostly warehouse & industrial properties located in the U.S. Subsequent to quarter's end management made an additional $170 million in investments.

Dispositions totaled $495.2 million with management stating cap rates right inside 6%. As a result of solid investments and capital recycling, WPC was able to raise full-year investment, disposition, & AFFO guidance.

Investments are now expected to be between $1.8 billion and $2.1 billion. More than $70 million in deals are expected to close in Q4. Dispositions are expected to be between $1.3 billion and $1.5 billion. AFFO is now projected in a range of $4.93 - $4.99, up from $4.87 - $4.95, representing solid growth of 5% from 2024.

Well-Covered Dividend

W.P. Carey ended the quarter with an AFFO payout of 73%. Solid for a net lease REITs. The lower the better but I typically look for REITs with a payout ratio around 75% or below. For comparison, Realty Income's (O) payout ratio was 77%, while Agree Realty's (ADC) was 71%.

WPC's management stated that free cash flow after dividends was $250 million on an annualized basis. This, plus their capital recycling, and forward equity raises gives the REIT plenty of retained capital to fund future investments. For 2026, WPC stated they expect $180 million in investments to come online.

Strong Financial Position

Despite having over $1 billion in debt maturing, WPC's debt ladder was solidly staggered. Total debt stood at $8.8 billion with a weighted-average interest rate of 3.2%.

Liquidity was strong with $2.1 billion and net debt to EBITDA ratio at 5.9x. They also raised $230 million in forward equity with their bond issuance at an average price of $68.05.

Their leverage was higher than peers like O & ADC. Realty Income's stood at 5.4x, while Agree Realty's was the lowest of the group at 5.1x. This is within management's targeted range of the mid to high 5x's.

WPC's first debt maturity is this April with $500 million in Euro bonds maturing. Their U.S. dollar bond isn't due in October of 2026. So, from a liquidity and balance sheet standpoint, WPC is in a comfortable position for solid acquisitions amidst declining interest rates.

Valuation

At their current price of $67.71, this gives WPC a forward P/AFFO multiple of 13.63x. This puts them below the peer average of 15.43x. While I do think near-term upside may be limited, I like where W.P. Carey seems headed despite lingering risks.

Moreover, their well-covered dividend yield of 5.32% and upside potential in the next 2 to 3 years is attractive if you're a long-term income-focused investor. Once we see more economic stability and rates come down significantly, I could see WPC moving closer to their high price target of $79.

Risks & Bottom Line

Although REITs look poised to benefit from a lower rate environment, WPC's tenant risks still linger, preferably from Hellweg. The REIT continues to take back assets from the retailer and re-tenanting efforts remain ongoing.

Hellweg is now WPC's 14th largest tenant, down from 6th in Q2. And management stated they expect this to continue declining into the top 25 by 2026. But a potential recession could result in slower activity as well as financial distress from additional tenants.

Because of this, W.P. Carey could continue to see limited upside and trade rangebound. Agree Realty, who currently has the highest premium amongst peers is likely a result of their investment-rated portfolio.

WPC's investment-grade tenant exposure stood at just 21.9%. And if we do see further economic weakening, WPC could experience further declines in occupancy, resulting in potential downside.

However, from a valuation and fundamental standpoint, W.P. Carey's upside over the next 2 to 3 years could be significant should we avoid a recession. If you're an income-focused investor looking for a solid long-term play with a high yield that's well-covered, then WPC should be considered despite me rating them a hold.

data.title: Gates Industrial: Structural Growth Underway, Yet The Stock Still Feels Cyclical
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Gates Industrial Corporation (GTES) is undergoing a structural shift that the market is still seeing as cyclical fluctuations. Rather than a mere legacy industrial components supplier, GTES is evolving to become a key enabling component in electrification, energy efficiency, and digital infrastructure, particularly in data centers and next-generation mobility. By refocusing its portfolio on high-value applications such as hyperscale cooling, e-mobility, and industrial automation, GTES is transforming the quality of its revenues, not merely the quantity.

GTES’ stock is still trading based on a late-cycle industrial with low growth characteristics, while its underlying business is beginning to resemble an early-stage platform for power transmission and thermal management in structural growth sectors. The market is discounting near-term cyclical uncertainties in industrial and off-highway demand, but failing to recognize the multi-year growth opportunities inherent in data center expansion, the electrification of transportation, and the retrofitting of facilities for greater efficiency.

While GTES’ margins have not yet reached their optimal levels, the trend of margin improvement is clearly established. The mix is improving, legacy low-return segments are being de-emphasized, and the benefits of post-spin cost reductions are still working their way through the system. With operating leverage returning and secular end-markets continuing to grow, GTES is poised for a period of accelerating earnings growth.

GTES is currently priced at a level that does not reflect the transformative changes underway within the company. Due to this disconnect, I consider GTES to be a buy opportunity.

About the Company

GTES is a global provider of power-transmission and fluid-power solutions used in industrial machinery, off-highway equipment, automotive systems, and increasingly in energy and digital-infrastructure applications. Throughout its 100+ year history, GTES has evolved from a rubber-and-belt producer into a diversified motion control manufacturer with manufacturing and distribution locations throughout North America, Europe, and Asia.

GTES operates through two segments: Power Transmission, which consists of belts, chains, and engineered polymer systems for industrial, automotive, and energy applications; and Fluid Power, which supplies hydraulic hoses, couplings, and systems for construction, agricultural, mining, and mobility platforms. As a result of a combination of original equipment manufacturers (OEM) and after-market customers, GTES has a stable revenue stream.

Recent Examples of New Applications

GTES recently introduced the Data Master™ Eco liquid-cooled hose designed for hyperscale data centers and high-performance computing applications. The new hose is approximately 15% lighter and was produced using a process that reportedly uses 75% less energy than previous processes.

Topline Analysis & Outlook

In my view, GTES' recent performance cannot simply be viewed through a lens of growth alone. Q3 FY25 revenue was $855.7mn (+1.7% core YoY) and 9M revenue was $2.59bn (+0.8% core YoY). What is more important is where stability is coming from and how the mix of GTES' products is changing. Due to high after-market exposure, price discipline, and a slow but visible transition into structurally more robust applications, including e-mobility and data infrastructure, GTES is experiencing a relatively stable topline in an environment where many parts of industrial capital expenditures have either remained stalled or have been very cautious. This is not cyclical resiliency; it is structural stabilization.

The Power Transmission segment of GTES' business is the backbone of GTES' business and the primary driver of GTES' stability during this cycle. GTES' Q3 FY25 segment revenue was $533.3mn, representing a 2.3% core YoY increase. 9M revenue for the Power Transmission segment was $1.61bn, representing a 1.6% core YoY increase. While GTES' headline growth appears modest, the nature of this growth is much more important than the actual rate of growth. A large portion of this segment is comprised of the replacement market, which exhibits fundamental behavior differences compared to OEM-based cycles. Demand for replacement products is independent of the level of industrial production and instead reflects the usage of an installed base, wear cycles, and maintenance schedules. Therefore, this segment has been resilient throughout the entire cycle in spite of global production levels remaining depressed.

While the baseline stability of the Power Transmission segment is providing a foundation for GTES' overall topline stability, the more notable trend is where incremental demand is developing within this segment. GTES is slowly increasing its participation in higher value-added applications, including those related to electrified mobility systems, industrial automation belts, and increasingly, thermal management solutions related to digital infrastructure. These are not isolated pockets of demand; instead, they represent structurally growing applications in which mechanical power transmission is essential regardless of the degree of electrification of the system. Through FY26-FY27, the Power Transmission segment will be less reliant upon a general industrial recovery and more aligned with underlying trends, including urban mobility, automated equipment replacement cycles, and energy efficiency upgrades. I would anticipate this segment to grow between low- to mid-single digits in FY26, and potentially reach the upper end of that range by FY27 as these themes continue to evolve and retrofit and replacement cycles strengthen.

Fluid Power is where GTES' topline appears to be weaker, yet is strategically more interesting. GTES' Q3 FY25 Fluid Power segment revenue was $322.4mn, representing a 0.7% core YoY increase. The 9M revenue for the Fluid Power segment was $976.4mn, representing a 0.6% core YoY decline. At first glance, this may suggest that the Fluid Power segment is a lagging indicator, and it is. The industrial hydraulics and mobile equipment demand in Fluid Power is weak, particularly in the construction and heavy machinery space. However, Fluid Power is also where GTES is creating the most future optionality. GTES is quietly repositioning the Fluid Power segment. Legacy hydraulics and fluid solutions in construction and agriculture remain cyclical, whereas the newer applications in electrified industrial systems and data center thermal management exhibit characteristics of structurally growing markets. Liquid cooling represents a product extension for GTES, but it also serves as a window into a structurally new revenue stream for the company, specifically hyperscale infrastructure and AI-driven data centers. Hyperscale data centers require high-performance hoses, couplings, and thermal management materials that align perfectly with the Fluid Power capabilities of GTES. I believe that GTES will face a transitional year for Fluid Power in FY26, with revenues fluctuating around flat to low single-digit growth as traditional industrial demand levels stabilize. However, by FY27, if industrial demand only partially recovers and GTES' exposure to data centers continues to grow, I believe that Fluid Power can reasonably grow in the low to mid single-digits, driven primarily by mix shifts rather than cyclical rebounds in legacy markets. Ultimately, the direction of travel for this segment is away from pure dependence on heavy industrial cycles and toward increased alignment with structurally growing markets of digital infrastructure and electrification.

Collectively, GTES' topline expectations for FY26-FY27 reflect a slow improvement in growth quality. The Power Transmission segment continues to generate stability based on the replacement-driven model, while Fluid Power gradually decreases its cyclicality through its increasing participation in structurally growing end markets. This mix provides the basis for GTES to achieve a low to mid single-digit growth trajectory over the next two years.

Margin Analysis & Outlook

GTES is currently in a phase of margin growth driven more by self-help and mix than by volume recovery. GTES reported an adj. EBITDA of $196 million on $856 million of revenue in Q3, resulting in an adj. EBITDA margin of 22.9% (+90 bps y/y). Through 9M, adj. EBITDA margin is ~22.5%, and the company expects to report full-year performance in the 22.5%-23.0% range. Assuming no material reversal in the cost environment, and given the company's current mix and cost structure, GTES' FY25 adj. EBITDA margin is likely to settle in this range rather than expand meaningfully. What is more important than the magnitude of the margin is the direction of travel for GTES' adj. EBITDA margins post-FY25.

The Power Transmission segment is the primary driver of GTES' profitability and margin stability. GTES reported an adj. EBITDA margin of ~22.9% for the Power Transmission segment in Q3, a 100 bps increase versus last year in spite of depressed OEM demand. GTES reports that the majority of the margin strength in the Power Transmission segment is due to mix improvements (increasingly higher share of aftermarket, mobility, and select specialty industrial applications), ongoing 80/20 SKU rationalization, and cost initiatives across materials and manufacturing footprint. Importantly, the fact that margins are trending toward 23% even in the absence of a volume increase suggests that margins will continue to be strong going forward. I expect that GTES' Power Transmission segment will remain in the low twenties in FY25, and then expand by 100-150 bps over FY26-FY27 as operating leverage begins to develop, and the impact of structural savings becomes more apparent.

The Fluid Power segment is exhibiting improving underlying profitability despite softer end-markets for heavy equipment and agricultural uses. Like the Power Transmission segment, the Fluid Power segment also exhibited a ~22.9% adj. EBITDA margin in Q3, an increase of ~900 bps year-on-year, in spite of depressed demand. The margin improvement in the Fluid Power segment is attributed to a combination of pricing discipline, footprint optimization, and a gradual shift toward higher-value applications, including data center cooling assemblies and more engineered fluid solutions. While volume recovery in the Fluid Power segment is uncertain in the near term, the cost base of the segment has clearly been reduced. I expect that the Fluid Power segment will maintain margins of ~22-23% in FY25, and then gradually move into the mid-20s range by FY27, primarily driven by mix and efficiency improvements rather than a significant rebound in volume.

On a consolidated basis, I believe that GTES' adjusted EBITDA margins will be structurally supported at levels greater than 24%. I expect that GTES' adjusted EBITDA margin will be in the 22.5-23.0% range for FY25, reflecting a full year of stabilized costs and pricing discipline.Thereafter, I expect a more measured expansion in margins (i.e., 100-120bps in FY26, and a further ~150bps plus in FY27) as GTES experiences the benefits of its self-help programs and as the company achieves operating leverage.

I believe that the quality of the margin recovery for GTES differs significantly from past cycles. Historically, GTES relied heavily on both volume and pricing increases to expand profitability. Conversely, GTES is rebuilding margins through structural cost reductions, product mix improvements, and footprint rationalizations.

Relative to its peers, GTES lies at the low end of the industrial peer group in terms of relative multiple metrics, even while delivering growth rates that are equal to and in some cases surpass those of its higher-rated peers. Based on fiscal year 2026 financial metrics, GTES shares are trading at a 13.1x forward price-to-earnings ratio and 10x enterprise value-to-earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization (EV/EBITDA) metric, compared to a peer average of 20x and 15.9x, respectively.

Conversely, the premium names: ITT (24x), Ingersoll Rand (22x), Parker Hannifin (26x), Eaton (24x), and Emerson (18x) are all trading substantially higher than GTES, despite generally similar mid-teens growth trajectories. When looking at the peer group on a price-to-earnings growth (PEG) basis, the disparity is even greater. At 0.9x, GTES is trading much below the peer group average of 1.7x, and is only close to Timken and Regal Rexnord. The rest of the group is trading in the range of ~1.8x to ~2.6x. This pricing is essentially treating GTES as a lower quality or higher risk industrial, although GTES' margin expansion in recent years and a better product mix indicate otherwise.

When conducting a discounted cash flow (DCF) analysis using a 7.8% weighted average cost of capital (WACC) (4.0% risk-free rate, 4.3% equity risk premium, 1.2x beta) and a 2.0% terminal growth rate, the DCF model generates an enterprise value of ~$8.9bn and an equity value of ~$7.1bn or $28-$30/share (~20-30% above the current $22.80).

In summary, GTES is trading at a significant discount to its peers on both the P/E and EV/EBITDA multiple bases, despite comparable growth, and DCF analysis shows that the current stock price does not accurately reflect the company's improving earnings and cash flow profile, which is the core of the Buy recommendation.

Risks

The most obvious risk to the GTES is that some of the parts of the cycle that it is involved in may take longer to normalise than expected. While GTES is no longer the pure industrial hose and belt company, a large portion of its revenue comes from the construction equipment, automotive replacement cycles, and industrial production. If global industrial activity is sluggish, especially in Europe and China, then volume recovery in Fluid Power may be more muted than presently expected, putting back the realization of operating leverage.

A second risk is in the pace of transformation in the GTES portfolio. The strategic move to position the company towards higher-value applications, such as data centers, energy transition, and complex engineered solutions, is the right thing to do. However, these end markets do not scale linearly. For example, hyperscale data center build programs normally occur in waves. Any delay in capital expenditures or customer programs would have a negative effect on the near-term growth rate of the higher margin portion of GTES' business.

If GTES has difficulty in integrating the benefits of capacity expansion, product re-mix, or supply chain optimization, then margin growth can be slower than it would otherwise be, even if demand is otherwise sustained.

These are not fatal flaws. The existence of a powerful aftermarket business, sticky OEM relationships, and a growing percentage of engineered solutions to commoditized products buffers the cyclicality inherent in GTES' business and provides a cushion against downturns in future cycles.

Conclusion

As outlined above, GTES is currently at an inflection point. GTES is no longer just a legacy industrial components company that has exposure to commodity cycles, but is also not yet valued like a structural compounder. The transition of GTES towards engineered solutions, data center exposure, and higher margin applications is real and proceeding, but the extent to which the company's earnings are reflecting this transition is still in the making.

Currently, GTES' stock is providing a combination of improving operating quality and valuation support, which is a unique and valuable combination for a company that is still mid-transition. As discussed previously, the DCF analysis shows that the market is discounting a conservative growth path for GTES compared to the company's actual earnings power five years out, assuming the margin growth path that the analyst outlines in the analysis and that the company maintains its discipline in executing on its strategy.

It is a revaluation thesis based on GTES' gradual improvement in structure, its continued attempt at rebalancing its portfolio, and the gradual increase of its ability to generate consistent cash flows. Therefore, in light of the positive risk-reward dynamic of GTES' medium-term prospects, I believe that it is reasonable to maintain a Buy rating, not solely due to GTES' near-term progress, but due to the company's potential long-term success.

Strategy

Capital Group International Core Equity ETF (CGIC) is an actively managed ex-U.S. large cap fund launched on 06/25/2024. CGIC has a portfolio of 173 stocks, a 30-day SEC yield of 1.93%, a 12-month trailing yield of 1.47%, and an expense ratio of 0.54%. Distributions are paid quarterly.

As described by Capital Group, the fund intends to invest at least 90% of its asset value in stocks listed outside the U.S., including emerging markets, that “have the potential for growth and/or to pay dividends.” It focuses on dividend-paying companies with strong earnings.

The fund is designed for investors seeking both capital appreciation and income.

The portfolio is divided into segments managed by eight individual managers who make investing decisions based on their professional judgment.
